content.text:
The pattern we're seeing in enterprise RAG deployments:

1. Team builds RAG system
2. Indexes internal knowledge base
3. Demo goes great
4. Security review happens
5. Deployment pauses

The security review question that stops everything:

"Can you show me which documents User A can access vs User B?"

Most teams can't answer this. Their RAG system treats all users identically.

This isn't a RAG limitation. It's an architecture gap.

The solution: permission-aware retrieval.

Evaluate access on every query. Same embeddings, different authorized results.

If your RAG deployment is stuck in security review, this is probably why.
content.headline: Why RAG Deployments Stall in Security Review
content.cta: Learn how to unblock your deployment

thread:
The enterprise RAG deployment timeline:

Week 1-4: Build and demo
Week 5: Security review
Week 6+: Stuck

Why? The question no one prepared for 🧵

1/ Security asks: "Can you show me which docs User A can see vs User B?"

Most RAG systems treat all users identically. The vector DB returns the same chunks regardless of who's asking.

2/ This isn't a feature gap. It's an architecture gap.

Vector DBs retrieve by semantic similarity.
They don't understand identity.
They don't evaluate permissions.

3/ The fix isn't metadata filters (require re-embedding).
It isn't prompt rules (not enforceable).

It's permission-aware retrieval: evaluate access on every query.

4/ Same question + different user = different authorized answer.

That's how enterprise RAG should work.

If you're stuck in security review, this is probably why.

entries.notes: Week 1: Security Gap - Deployment pattern thread

content.text:
Your RAG system doesn't have a "view as user" feature.

This is a problem.

Without it, you can't:
→ Debug access issues
→ Validate policy changes
→ Demo to security teams
→ Respond to audit requests

"We trust it works" isn't an answer when compliance asks.

Enterprise AI systems need observability. Not just for performance—for access control.

You should be able to:
1. Impersonate any user
2. Run any query
3. See exactly what would return
4. Understand why each decision was made

This is what Access Simulator does. Test policies before they affect production.
content.headline: Why Your RAG System Needs Access Observability

content.text:
Does your RAG system have a "view as user" feature?

If not, how do you:
- Debug access issues
- Validate policy changes
- Respond to audits

"We trust it works" isn't an enterprise answer.

The most dangerous AI deployments are the ones that "just work."

No errors. No obvious issues. Users are happy.

But under the surface:

→ A sales rep can query engineering roadmaps
→ A contractor can access employee records
→ An intern can see board meeting summaries

The AI doesn't know these are problems. It's doing exactly what it was designed to do: find semantically relevant content.

The absence of visible errors doesn't mean the system is secure.

It means security issues are invisible.

This is why permission-aware retrieval isn't optional for enterprise. It's the difference between "works" and "works correctly."
content.headline: The Danger of AI Systems That Just Work

content.text:
The most dangerous AI deployments are the ones that "just work."

No errors. Users happy.

But a sales rep can query engineering roadmaps.
A contractor can access employee records.

The AI doesn't know these are problems.

Question for teams deploying internal AI assistants:

How do you handle document-level permissions?

Curious what approaches are working (or not working) in practice.

No judgment—genuinely learning from what teams are doing.
content.headline: RAG Permissions Poll

content.text:
Policy patterns for enterprise RAG access control:

1. Department Boundaries
→ Engineering docs to Engineering team
→ HR docs to HR team
→ Simple RBAC, easy to implement

2. Project Isolation
→ Project A docs to Project A members
→ Requires relationship-based access (ReBAC)
→ Scales with org structure

3. Clearance Levels
→ Access if user.clearance >= doc.classification
→ Attribute-based access (ABAC)
→ Good for regulated industries

4. Hybrid Policies
→ Department boundaries + project overrides
→ Most realistic for complex orgs
→ Requires policy composition

Gateco supports all four. Define policies in Policy Studio, evaluated at query time.

Which pattern matches your org?
content.headline: Policy Patterns for RAG Access Control

thread: 4 policy patterns for enterprise RAG access control 🧵

1/ Department Boundaries

Engineering → Engineering docs
HR → HR docs

Simple RBAC. Easy to implement. Works for most orgs.

2/ Project Isolation

Project A → Project A docs only

Requires relationship-based access (ReBAC).
Scales with org structure.

3/ Clearance Levels

user.clearance >= doc.classification

Attribute-based access (ABAC).
Good for regulated industries.

4/ Hybrid Policies

Department boundaries + project overrides.

Most realistic for complex orgs.
Requires policy composition.

Which pattern matches your org?

content.text:
Integrating Gateco with LlamaIndex:

```python
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex
from gateco import GatecoRetriever

# Initialize Gateco retriever
retriever = GatecoRetriever(
    api_key="gco_...",
    user_context=lambda: get_current_user()
)

# Create index with Gateco retriever
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(
    documents,
    retriever=retriever
)

# Query with permission-aware retrieval
query_engine = index.as_query_engine()
response = query_engine.query("What's the project status?")
```

Every query now:
→ Checks user identity
→ Evaluates access policies
→ Filters unauthorized chunks
→ Logs the retrieval decision

Same LlamaIndex patterns. Added security layer.

5-minute integration. Full documentation available.

Policy versioning for RAG access control:

Why it matters:

1. Audit Trail
→ "What policy was active when this retrieval happened?"
→ Critical for compliance investigations

2. Rollback Capability
→ New policy causing issues? Revert instantly
→ No re-embedding required

3. Testing Before Deployment
→ Preview policy v2 in Access Simulator
→ See impact before it affects users

4. Change Documentation
→ Who changed what, when, why
→ Required for SOC 2 controls

Gateco tracks every policy version. Changes apply instantly—no reprocessing.

Access Simulator lets you test any version before deployment.

Policies should be versioned like code.
content.headline: Policy Versioning for RAG

content.text:
Policies should be versioned like code.

✓ Know what was active at any time
✓ Rollback instantly if needed
✓ Test before deployment
✓ Document every change

This isn't optional for enterprise RAG.

Multi-model RAG architectures and access control:

Common pattern:
→ Multiple specialized indexes for different use cases
→ Router chooses which index to query
→ Results aggregated

The security challenge:
→ Each index may have different access rules
→ User may have access to Index A but not Index B
→ Router needs to know permissions before routing

Solution:
1. Pre-routing authorization check
2. Filter eligible indexes by user
3. Route to authorized subset only
4. Log all routing decisions

Gateco supports multi-index configurations.

Define policies per index. Routing respects permissions.

Same user, different authorized indexes.
content.headline: Multi-Model RAG Security

content.text:
Multi-cloud AI deployments and data residency:

Challenge:
→ Data must stay in specific regions (GDPR, local regulations)
→ AI systems span multiple cloud providers
→ Vector databases may be centralized

The question: Where does authorization happen?

❌ At the application layer (inconsistent across clouds)
❌ At the vector DB (centralized = data movement)
❌ Per-provider solutions (fragmented policy)

✅ At the retrieval layer (cloud-agnostic, consistent)

Gateco provides:
→ Consistent policy across AWS, GCP, Azure
→ Data stays where it is
→ Authorization distributed to edge
→ Single policy source of truth

Multi-cloud AI requires cloud-agnostic security.
content.headline: Multi-Cloud RAG Security

thread:
Multi-cloud AI deployments have a data residency problem.

Data must stay in specific regions.
AI systems span providers.
Vector DBs may be centralized.

Where does authorization happen? 🧵

1/ At the application layer?

❌ Inconsistent across clouds
❌ Each app implements differently
❌ Hard to audit

2/ At the vector DB?

❌ Centralized = data movement
❌ Violates residency requirements
❌ Single point of failure

3/ Per-provider solutions?

❌ Fragmented policy
❌ Drift between clouds
❌ Operational nightmare

4/ At the retrieval layer ✅

→ Cloud-agnostic
→ Consistent policy everywhere
→ Data stays in place
→ Single source of truth

This is what Gateco provides.

content.text:
AI audits are getting more sophisticated.

What auditors used to ask:
→ "Do you use AI?"
→ "What data does it access?"

What auditors ask now:
→ "Show me the access control policy for your AI retrieval."
→ "Demonstrate different users getting different results."
→ "Provide audit logs for AI data access in Q1."
→ "How do you handle permission changes?"

The bar has moved from "do you have controls" to "prove the controls work."

If your RAG system can't demonstrate these capabilities, you have an audit finding waiting to happen.

Gateco provides:
→ Policy demonstration via Access Simulator
→ Exportable audit logs (SIEM-ready)
→ Instant permission changes (no re-embedding)

Be audit-ready before the audit.
content.headline: AI Audit Requirements Are Evolving

content.text:
What auditors used to ask about AI:
"Do you use AI?"

What they ask now:
"Demonstrate different users getting different results from your AI."

The bar moved from "have controls" to "prove they work."

Is your RAG system ready?

Customer-managed encryption keys for AI retrieval:

Why it matters:

1. Data Sovereignty
→ You control the keys
→ Provider can't access content without your key
→ Required for some industries

2. Key Rotation
→ Rotate keys without re-embedding
→ Comply with key lifecycle policies
→ No downtime

3. Audit Trail
→ Know who accessed what, when
→ Key usage logged separately from data access
→ Complete chain of custody

4. Emergency Response
→ Revoke key = immediate data inaccessibility
→ Faster than deleting embeddings
→ Crisis containment

Gateco Enterprise supports BYOK (Bring Your Own Key).

Your keys. Your control. Our security layer.

content.text:
May recap:

🔐 Security gap deep-dives
🔧 LlamaIndex integration
📋 Multi-cloud compliance
🚀 Policy templates library

Coming in June:
→ AI agent authorization
→ Customer stories
→ Advanced policies

What should we cover?

AI agents are coming to your enterprise.

Not copilots with human-in-the-loop.

Autonomous agents that:
→ Traverse multiple data sources
→ Execute actions without asking
→ Make retrieval decisions independently

The security model is fundamentally different:

1. No user session
→ Agent acts on behalf of a principal
→ But makes its own decisions

2. Scope must be constrained
→ What can this agent access?
→ Not what can the user access

3. Complete audit trail
→ What did the agent actually do?
→ Not just what the user asked

Agent authorization isn't user authorization.

It's delegated authority with constraints.

More on this in June.
content.headline: AI Agent Authorization
